message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Inferring the impact of climate upon the transmission of SARS-CoV-2 has been confounded by variability in testing, unknown disease introduction rates, and changing weather. Here we present a data model that accounts for dynamic testing rates and variations in disease introduction rates. We apply this model to data from Colombia, whose varied and seasonless climate, central port of entry, and swift, centralized response to the Covid-19 pandemic present an opportune environment for assessing the impact of climate factors on the spread of Covid-19. We observe strong attenuation of transmission in climates with sustained daily temperatures above 30 degrees Celsius and simultaneous mean relative humidity below 78%, with outbreaks occurring at high humidity even where the temperature is high. We hypothesize that temperature and relative humidity comodulate the infectivity of SARS-CoV-2 within respiratory droplets.</jats:p>
title: The transmission of SARS-CoV-2 is likely comodulated by temperature and by relative humidity
